title: A Barber Shop of Mixed Clientele
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Library of Congress in December 2015, Timothy Jenkins opens on his birth in Philadelphia in 1938, his father's barber shop with its mix of professionals and laypeople, his maternal grandfather William Albert Jones who served on the school board and founded a Black Republican group called Remember John Brown, and his paternal grandfather who founded a Rosenwald school in Courtland, Virginia.

title: Reverend O.G. Jenkins of Courtland
summary: Jenkins describes the Remember John Brown organization that supported Black businesses and education in Philadelphia, the photograph of his paternal grandfather Reverend O.G. Jenkins seated before the Courtland Normal and Industrial Institute, and how he only later discovered as an adult that this grandfather had also founded Hood Memorial Church in Richmond, the largest African American Zion Episcopal church there.

title: From Thaddeus Stevens to a Howard Scholarship
summary: He recounts his education at the Thaddeus Stevens School of Practice, a teacher-training facility with a heavily integrated student body and extensive museum field trips that left him fascinated by the Philadelphia Museum of Art's Scholars Room, and how a recruiter at Central High School won him a full tuition scholarship to Howard, reinforced by family ties to historically Black colleges and a neighbor whose sister was Howard's dean of women.

title: Rayford Logan and Sterling Brown
summary: Entering Howard in 1956, Jenkins describes a stimulating campus of debate and philosophy clubs and a faculty steeped in Black social action: historian Rayford Logan, general secretary of the Pan-African movement under W.E.B. Du Bois, and the poet and dialect critic Sterling Brown, who opened his home to students like Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox, and Jean Wheeler and whose father may have started the first correspondence school for Black ministers.

title: The Little Forum
summary: Jenkins honors political scientist Harold Lewis and sociologist E. Franklin Frazier, author of the study of the Black bourgeoisie, and describes the Little Forum, a monthly reading club in Cook Hall where a student and a faculty member each presented one book as peers, the one setting where student opinion was weighted as heavily as faculty and a melting pot for formal study and current events.

title: The Granite Bench Behind Founders
summary: As student body president, Jenkins gained access to Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, Howard's first Black president, who during the McCarthy era refused to dismiss faculty pursued by the House Un-American Activities Committee and would not expel students like Pauli Murray, arrested for a 1940s sit-in at the House of Representatives cafeteria; Jenkins waylaid him on the granite bench behind Founders Library for informal talks the secretary would never schedule.

title: A More Radical Dimension
summary: Jenkins describes the campus grievances Howard students pressed before the 1960s and how off-campus activism came through his selection as Howard's delegate to the United States National Student Association and the CIA-linked International Student Relations Seminar at Harvard, where he met students like future congressman Barney Frank and the Students for a Democratic Society, just as the Greensboro sit-in erupted toward the end of his term as student body president.

title: The Howard Fervor
summary: Jenkins points to a tradition of Howard protest against segregated department stores and drinking fountains in the 1940s and 1950s, and to Jonathan Holloway's book on Howard, which treats E. Franklin Frazier, Ralph Bunche, and economist Abram Harris and the faculty's conflicts with the administration, showing Howard's nexus with nearly every aspect of African American political activity.

title: Compelled Contributions
summary: In his final year the sit-ins erupted and the student council voted to support the legal defense of arrested students, but the administration barred using compulsory student funds for off-campus political activity; Jenkins describes the twist of funding transportation to a convention in Canada instead, and Dean Armour Blackburn's opposition overruled by Mordecai Johnson.

title: A Man Among Boys
summary: Graduating in June 1960, Jenkins notes that NAACP chapters had run sit-ins long before 1960, and recalls that as he left, Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox, Dion Diamond, and Jean Wheeler entered as freshmen, the future stalwarts of SNCC; Carmichael's memoir Ready for Revolution devotes nearly a hundred pages to Howard and credits Jenkins's handoff, calling him a man among boys.

title: The Nonviolent Action Group
summary: Jenkins recounts being elected National Affairs Vice President of the United States National Student Association the year after graduating, returning often to Howard, where the new Nonviolent Action Group had formed to integrate lunch counters and amusement parks in Washington, and his place on the executive committees of both SNCC and SDS, though he does not recall attending the Shaw University founding meeting.

title: Preempted by Acclamation
summary: Jenkins describes his original aim to run for International Affairs Vice President, his trips to Cuba and his hosting of African and Caribbean students, and how he learned only later that the CIA funded the International Student Relations Seminar and ran operatives in the NSA; distrusting his civil rights ties, they maneuvered to name him National Affairs Vice President by acclamation, blocking his international bid.

title: A Slave-Owning Fascist Mentality
topic: NSA Battles Over Integration and the Right
summary: He explains how the NSA had passed integration resolutions since Brown v. Board of Education over Southern objection, and how the sit-in resolution he supported cost the association many Southern schools who walked out, then recounts confronting the rising right-wing Young Americans for Freedom by rebutting their invited speaker William F. Buckley Jr. as a slave-owning fascist mentality, prompting a censure motion he survived.

part: Ella Baker and the SNCC Divide
title: The Godmother of SNCC
summary: Jenkins describes Ella Baker as a great eminence of civil rights, tied to the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters and A. Philip Randolph, instrumental in founding the Southern Christian Leadership Conference, and sheltered by the YWCA, who became the godmother of SNCC; he was surprised to find in old minutes that he chaired the December executive committee meeting at Atlanta's Butler Street YMCA where Marion Barry was replaced by Chuck McDew.

part: Ella Baker and the SNCC Divide
title: A Northern Voice for SNCC
summary: He explains that the Freedom Rides came in 1961 as he was leaving the NSA and enrolling in law school, so he never rode but supported them through Northern fundraising, and recounts Yale chaplain William Sloane Coffin Jr.'s participation and the funds Coffin raised through the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom that Jenkins later carried to Medgar Evers's widow.
keywords: Women's International League for Peace and Freedom

part: Ella Baker and the SNCC Divide
title: The Religious and the Secular
summary: Jenkins frames the division of emphasis within SNCC between the religious Nashville contingent influenced by Jim Lawson, Diane Nash, and James Bevel, who treated direct action and nonviolence as Christian expression, and those like himself drawn to the politics of integration as civic responsibility rather than religious witness.

part: Ella Baker and the SNCC Divide
title: The Organs of State
summary: He recounts the danger of SNCC splitting along religious and secular lines and Ella Baker's role in harmonizing them, and lays out his own conviction, shared with Chuck McDew and Charles Jones, that sit-ins and pray-ins were cosmetic and that real change required taking control of the organs of state that determined the police, the roads, the budgets, and public employment, borrowing Nkrumah's Consciencism and the gospel call to seek first the political kingdom.

part: Ella Baker and the SNCC Divide
title: We Were Co-opting Them
summary: Jenkins describes the meeting at Capahosic, Virginia where Kennedy administration operatives sought to steer the movement from volatile street demonstrations toward voter registration, which carried more constitutional justification for federal involvement, and disputes the historians' claim, including Howard Zinn's, that SNCC was co-opted, arguing SNCC co-opted the administration into launching a south-wide voter education campaign attended by Charles Jones, Charles Sherrod, Chuck McDew, and Burke Marshall.

title: Local Draft Boards
summary: Jenkins explains that local draft boards reflecting Mississippi's politicians used their authority to target SNCC and civil rights activists for the draft, and recalls the National Conference of Black Lawyers later challenging General Lewis Hershey's pursuit of activists, including a case where a student over twenty-three was imprisoned despite the statute and the conviction was thrown out.

title: A Three-Judge Panel
summary: He describes SNCC's major contribution in the voting rights testimony, forcing an amendment so voting cases were heard by a three-judge panel rather than a single segregationist district judge, with two of three judges named by the chief judge of the Fifth Circuit, where Eisenhower-appointed Republican judges allowed most such cases to be won over Kennedy's opposition.

part: The First Freedom School and Highlander
title: A Cram Course in History
summary: Jenkins recounts organizing a seminar in Nashville in the summer of 1961 to replicate Howard's Little Forum, inviting sit-in leaders to study Black history and the politics of race with top minds, funded by the New World and Taconic Foundations with letters of support from Dr. King and Ella Baker, which he calls the first Freedom School, and notes Nashville was chosen for Fisk University's Race Relations Institute rather than the religious persuasion of Lawson or Nash.

part: The First Freedom School and Highlander
title: Pushed Off the Highway
summary: Jenkins recalls the many meetings at Highlander Folk School and a drive from Monteagle toward Chattanooga when a truck driver, seeing an integrated group singing in a Volkswagen bus, pushed them off the mountain highway, their fall halted by a tree above the ravine until a pickup truck pulled them out; he remembers Ella Baker, skeptical of ministers, motioning him close on her stretcher to say, thank God for Jesus.

title: Out of the Reach of Disaster
summary: Jenkins explains his dual purpose in entering Yale law school that fall: to gain skills relevant to social change, and to serve as one of the SNCC people kept out of reach of murder, incarceration, or the draft so the organization could be rebuilt if its Southern projects were wiped out overnight, a role he likens to Che Guevara returning from the Sierra Maestra.

title: The Northern Student Movement
summary: He describes Yale as a northern adjunct to SNCC, where he organized Freedom Singers tours and recruited William Sloane Coffin Jr. as a freedom rider, and where he met Peter and Joan Countryman, founders of the Northern Student Movement, which did in the North what SNCC did in the South and which he counts among the unsung heroes of the era.

title: The Backbone of Voter Registration
summary: Jenkins notes other satellite student groups like the Young Christian Students and SDS that aided the Southern movement, and the June 1961 letter in which Ella Baker introduced him to Bob Moses, Amzie Moore, and the people who became the backbone of voter registration, as SNCC chose McComb, rural and isolated, to be a catalyst for a statewide effort.

title: Nobody Wanted McComb
summary: He explains that while the NAACP wanted Atlanta, SCLC Montgomery, and CORE Louisiana, nobody wanted McComb, so SNCC went into one of the most hostile and remote areas to make a beachhead, relying on Ella Baker, Amzie Moore, and Bob Moses for contacts and on a Black Masonic lodge the police would not defile to house meetings there and in Jackson.

title: Welcome to the Magnolia State
summary: Jenkins tells of driving from Chicago to McComb with Chuck McDew, Charles Sherrod, and Charles Jones when they hit a dog in a small Alabama town, a hostile crowd forming to demand compensation, until an elderly white man in a white linen suit and straw hat parted the crowd, said the boys could not have avoided it, then told them to keep quiet and get out, after which they drove without stopping into Mississippi.

title: It's Still There
summary: He assesses McComb by its survival, with students still doing civil rights projects, scholarship programs, and a Black history curriculum, and credits Teaching for Change as the institutional arm that made McComb a poster child for what comes from working with teachers and parents to deepen a community's grasp of its own history and social engagement.

title: Not Letting Herbert Lee Die in Vain
summary: Jenkins points to the murder of Herbert Lee by a state representative as a teaching tool he used in a 1962 or 1963 Holmes County speech, and to Bob Moses living among the people in an intellectual guerrilla fashion to build a movement from the ground up, the model Teaching for Change carries forward through its website and ties to civil rights veterans and the One Person One Vote organization.

title: Defenders of Those Who Did Want to Test
summary: He recalls being filmed rabble-rousing and singing alongside Bob Dylan, Pete Seeger, and Theodore Bikel in Greenwood, where Mrs. McGhee defended her farm with firearms against the Klan and local people with no prior preparation for politics took up voter registration, and where even those who could not pass the literacy test drove others to register, captured in the short film The Streets of Greenwood.

title: Turn Us Around
summary: Jenkins describes the song line about not letting Laurie Pritchett turn them around and Albany, Georgia, where Charles Sherrod chose neglected Southwest Georgia with its heavy Black population, mobilizing a grassroots organization in Ella Baker's spirit; he calls Albany the most ferocious expression of police-sanctioned violence in the country, where Dr. King and SCLC eventually joined the demonstrations.

title: A Slow Boiling Success
summary: Responding to scholars and SCLC voices who call the Albany Movement a failure, Jenkins argues it depends on expectations, that Albany was a rural area off the side roads of history with no celebrated victories, yet a tremendous success as people kept their homes through farmers' home support and learned to navigate the agriculture department, illustrated when Charles Sherrod's wife Shirley became a Department of Agriculture administrator in the area.

title: The Greenwood Folk Festival
summary: He describes the Greenwood folk festival on Mrs. McGhee's farm, intended to dispute the notion that the South had no culture and to let the larger entertainment community show support, drawing names like Seeger, Dylan, Joan Baez, and Bikel, and traces SNCC's mobilization through song to the Jubilee Singers of Fisk and the Howard Gospel Singers.

title: We Shall Overcome
summary: Jenkins recounts that We Shall Overcome was written as a religious ballad by Charles Albert Tindley, a Methodist minister in his hometown of Philadelphia, and turned into a political anthem with Guy Carawan, Pete Seeger, and the Freedom Singers, and describes how the songs gave demonstrators solidarity and courage in jail, so deeply connected to the movement that SNCC veterans still grow misty-eyed singing them.

title: First to the Widow's Door
summary: Jenkins recalls being a law student in New Haven when Medgar Evers was assassinated, and how William Sloane Coffin Jr. and the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom, a group on the House Un-American Activities Committee list, raised money so he could travel to Jackson and be the first person from out of state to bring a check to Evers's door.
keywords: Women's International League for Peace and Freedom

title: Stop in the Name of Medgar
summary: He distinguishes the public and private sides of the June 1963 Jackson march after Evers's funeral, when youngsters broke from the police-escorted procession and a confrontation followed, captured in Eyes on the Prize; privately, told that armed Black veterans on a rooftop had John Doar in their telescopic sights, mistaking him for the police, Jenkins reached the roof and cried, stop in the name of Medgar, and they stopped.

title: Take It to King's House
summary: Jenkins names his three brushes with history, the first Freedom School, the John Doar roof, and a second moment in Atlanta when the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights planned a demonstration at Rich's department store against the wishes of a Black leadership establishment, including Martin Luther King Sr.; as a final speaker he urged not only going forward but routing the march to King's house to ask him to come out.
keywords: Committee on Appeal for Human Rights

title: Equidistant Between Kennedy and Nixon
topic: The Arrest That Moved an Election
summary: He recounts that King joined the demonstration and was arrested two days before the 1960 election, that Robert Kennedy intervened and a telegram of support went to Coretta King, that the United Auto Workers spread the story in leaflets across the country, and that with the Black vote equidistant between Kennedy and Nixon, some 250,000 unprepared voters turned out for Kennedy, who won by 100,000.

title: The Guiding Spirit, William Higgs
summary: Asked whether the Atlanta demonstration tied to the October 1960 SNCC conference, Jenkins is unsure, then turns to William L. Higgs, a native Mississippian and Ole Miss and Harvard Law graduate who made his career in civil rights litigation and was the guiding spirit behind the three-judge-panel amendment, defending Black lawyers degraded in the court of Judge William Harold Cox, an appointee of Senator James Eastland.

title: A Reconstruction-Era Hook
summary: Jenkins describes working with Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party president Lawrence Guyot and fellow Yale law student Bill Wood, finding in the Mississippi Code a provision originally meant to protect white people excluded by the Republican Party during Reconstruction that allowed protest votes, challenges, and independent elections, the foundation for the MFDP's mock elections and eventual participation in the Democratic Party.

title: The Voter Education Project
summary: He recounts statewide voter mobilization after SNCC through the Black leadership forum, the unions, and the Voter Education Project headed by Leo Branton and then Vernon Jordan, which channeled tax-exempt philanthropy into registration, and notes that the project refused to enter Mississippi as too hot, leaving Bob Moses and SNCC on their own in McComb and Greenwood, making their intervention all the more important.

title: Locked in His Room With Gandhi
summary: Citing Larry Rivers's article on the Rankin Chapel connection to King, Jenkins describes how Mordecai Johnson, mentor and devotee of Gandhi whose vice president William Stuart Nelson lived in India, spoke in Philadelphia while King was a student at Crozer, and how King, hearing him, locked himself in his room for ten days reading Gandhi and credited Johnson rather than Thoreau or Emerson, insisting the techniques and DNA of SNCC carry into the women's, disability, and poor people's movements.

title: The National Conference of Black Lawyers
summary: He records the National Conference of Black Lawyers, founded in 1968 on a call from Floyd McKissick to move the Black bar beyond civil rights into economic development, poverty, and housing law, naming judges and lawyers like Leon Higginbotham, Nathaniel Jones, Bruce Wright, and Raymond Pace Alexander, who won the Girard College case barring discrimination in charitable organizations.

title: Mayor for Life
summary: Jenkins explains the strong SNCC presence in Washington through Marion Barry, who as a student wrote him a four-page letter on Black people taking over local political structures, and who as mayor integrated every level of hierarchy, advanced women, and funded neglected areas, tracing the same lineage to Harold Washington in Chicago and to lawyers shaped by the Black Panthers, Attica, and the Soledad Brothers.

part: From the Street to the Suite
title: Reversing Woodrow Wilson
summary: Reflecting on his career, Jenkins recounts serving by appointment of President Carter on the Postal Service governing board, where as chair of the Corporate Responsibility Committee he reversed the segregation Woodrow Wilson had imposed across the federal government, brought in Kenneth Clark, and restored Amzie Moore's pension, then describes consulting for the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, teaching law, serving as president of the University of the District of Columbia, and building WHUT-TV.

part: From the Street to the Suite
title: Into the Belly of the Beast
summary: Jenkins recalls writing John Lewis in SNCC's twilight that its people should professionally infiltrate the machinery created by the legislation they drafted, migrating into the EEOC, the National Labor Relations Board, and the Federal Reserve, and laments that unlike labor the movement did not put its own people forward to administer its laws, a shortcoming he frames not as selling out but as a career mission.

part: From the Street to the Suite
title: The Next SNCC
summary: Jenkins closes on the failure, amid black power's egotism and myopia, to keep alliances with progressive whites and to mobilize the poor white community to its real interests, which he ties to today's red-state politics and Kentucky voters rejecting health care to avoid association with Obama, and to the unfinished human-rights mission, recalling the Southern Negro Youth Congress as the first SNCC and hoping for another to come.
